beats.text: Random cross-validation poisons a time series, because shuffled folds let the model train on the future to predict the past.

visual.sub: Train on the past, test on the future, never the reverse

beats.text: Walk-forward validation fixes a horizon, trains up to an origin, forecasts ahead, scores it, then slides the origin forward and repeats.
The rolling-origin backtest error averages a per-point loss across every horizon step and across every forecast origin.

visual.note: average over all origins and horizon steps

beats.text: An expanding window keeps all history when the process is stationary, while a sliding window forgets stale data when the process drifts.
Leave a gap between train and test when features look back or labels arrive late, so information cannot bleed across the seam.
Refit the model at every origin if you can afford it, since frozen parameters quietly over-state how stable the model really is.

visual.label: horizon h in the runnable backtest

beats.text: A backtest gives you errors, and a metric turns them into one comparable number, but every metric carries its own failure mode.

visual.sub: MAPE, MASE, sMAPE and where each one breaks

beats.text: MAPE averages absolute error as a fraction of the actual value, so being off by ten on a hundred reads as ten percent.

visual.note: scale-free but explodes near zero and is asymmetric

beats.text: A forecast of ninety against an actual of one hundred scores a ten percent MAPE on that single point.
MASE divides the model's mean absolute error by the naive forecast's error, making it unitless and defined even when actuals hit zero.

visual.note: below 1 beats naive, above 1 loses to copy-the-last-value

beats.text: A MASE of zero point seven means the model is about thirty percent more accurate than copying the last value forward.
Symmetric MAPE puts both actual and forecast in the denominator, but it is still mildly biased and undefined when both reach zero.

visual.note: each term bounded in 0 to 200 percent

beats.text: A single point forecast is a lie of omission, since decisions about safety stock and capital hinge on the downside, not the median.

visual.sub: Point forecasts are decent, intervals are usually too narrow

beats.text: Under Gaussian errors the interval is a z-band around the point forecast, using 1.96 for ninety-five percent and 1.645 for ninety.

visual.note: sigma grows with the horizon h

beats.text: Forecast uncertainty compounds with horizon, so for a random walk the band widens as sigma times the square root of h.
An interval is calibrated only if the truth lands inside it close to the nominal rate, so measure empirical coverage directly.

visual.note: compare empirical coverage to the nominal level

beats.text: If a model thinks sigma is zero point seven of the truth, its nominal ninety-five percent band covers only about eighty-three percent.
Calibrated bands come from empirical quantiles of backtest residuals or from conformal prediction with finite-sample guarantees.

concepts.title: ML & DL for time series — Prophet, DeepAR, TFT

beats.text: Classical ARIMA and exponential smoothing are hard to beat on a single series, but machine learning earns its keep across many.

visual.sub: Prophet, DeepAR, and the Temporal Fusion Transformer

beats.text: Prophet is a decomposable additive model of trend, seasonality, holidays, and noise, fit in a Bayesian framework for legible baselines.
DeepAR trains one autoregressive RNN across all series and outputs distribution parameters, giving calibrated prediction intervals for free.
The Temporal Fusion Transformer separates static, known-future, and observed-past inputs and reads off quantiles via pinball loss.
Four families trade interpretability against scale, from per-series ARIMA up to the covariate-rich attention transformer.
The M4 competition was won by a hybrid and M5 by gradient-boosted trees, so backtest any foundation model before believing the leaderboard.

visual.sub: Deep models are not free wins

beats.text: Almost every forecasting disaster traces to one cause: the offline score used information the model would never have had in production.

visual.sub: The model looks brilliant, then falls apart on day one

beats.text: Scaling on the full series bakes future statistics into training features, so fit every scaler inside each fold on past data only.
Late-arriving data leaks the target, since a revised figure known only after the outcome is not available at forecast time.
Tuning hyper-parameters on the reported period is the slowest leak, hiding in your workflow rather than in your code.
Even a clean backtest is perishable, because regimes change and a pandemic can rewrite every demand curve overnight.
The one rule that prevents most of this is to simulate production exactly and report MASE against the naive forecast.

visual.sub: What did I actually know at this instant?
